children.value: Very efficient operations (much more efficient than plain python)
Stackoverflow Blog: Why is Python Growing So Quickly?

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="numpy.dtype size changed")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="numpy.ufunc size changed")
 
# create a pandas Series
series_a = pd.Series([0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])
series_a
can be created from most python collections.
This also means that they support all content types that python collections support.

# a list
list_a = ['one','two','three']

series_b = pd.Series(list_a)
series_b

# a list with indices
list_a = ['one','two','three']
list_b = ['index_one','index_two','index_three']

series_c = pd.Series(data=list_a, index=list_b)
series_c

text/plain.content:
index_one        one
index_two        two
index_three    three
dtype: object

children.value:
# creating Series with same value
pd.Series(5, index=[100, 200, 300])
There are many reasons why these indexed structures are helpful. Most of them are related to speed. But many are related to convenience:

fruits = pd.Series([1,0,2,2,9], index=['apples','oranges','bananas','lemons', 'avocado'])
more_fruits = pd.Series([1,0,1,5], index=['lemons','oranges','apples', 'bananas'])

fruits + more_fruits

text/plain.content:
apples     2.0
avocado    NaN
bananas    7.0
lemons     3.0
oranges    0.0
dtype: float64

apples     1
oranges    0
bananas    2
lemons     2
avocado    9
dtype: int64

lemons     1
oranges    0
apples     1
bananas    5
dtype: int64

children.value: Pandas objects can be indexed in different ways:
explicit indices: what you define as index
Depending on how you access values in a
object, explicit or positional indexing is used.
To avoid confusion, specify the type of indexing with

data = pd.Series(['a', 'b', 'c'], index=[1, 3, 5])
# explicit index when indexing
data[1]

data = pd.Series(['a', 'b', 'c'], index=[1, 3, 5])
# explicit index when fancy-indexing
data[[1,3]]

data = pd.Series(['a', 'b', 'c'], index=[1, 3, 5])
# positional index when slicing
data[1:3]

data = pd.Series(['a', 'b', 'c'], index=['1', '3', '5'])
# positional index when actual index is not integer valued
data[1]

# apply custom functions

def some_function(v):
    '''
    Divides number by a million and returns its integer representation
    '''
    return int(v / 1e6)

some_function(35 * 1e6)

children.value: A matrix of data, passing optional row and column labels
dict of arrays, lists, or tuples
Each sequence becomes a column in the DataFrame; all sequences must be the same length
Each inner dict becomes a column
Each item becomes a row in the DataFrame
The DataFrame’s indexes are used unless different ones are passed
Like the “2D ndarray” case except masked values become NA/missing in the DataFrame result

# a DataFrame from a csv file
import os
df_from_csv = pd.read_csv(os.path.join("data","berlin_population.csv"))
Example: Getting Berlin Population Statistics from Wikipedia

children.label: Example: Getting Berlin Population Statistics from Wikipedia

children.value:
import requests
from io import StringIO

url = 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berlin_population_statistics'

# pretend to be a web browser
header = {
  "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/50.0.2661.75 Safari/537.36",
  "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest"
}

r = requests.get(url, headers=header)
df_berlin_population = pd.read_html(StringIO(r.text), header=0)
df_berlin_population[0]

children.value: Some Experiments with Names in Berlin

children.label: Some Experiments with Names in Berlin

children.value:
import urllib
import os

basedir = os.path.join("data","vornamen")
os.makedirs(basedir, exist_ok=True)

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/berlin/haeufige-vornamen-berlin/main/data/{}/{}.csv"

boroughs = [
"charlottenburg-wilmersdorf",
"friedrichshain-kreuzberg",
"lichtenberg",
"marzahn-hellersdorf",
"mitte",
"neukoelln",
"pankow",
"reinickendorf",
"spandau",
"steglitz-zehlendorf",
"tempelhof-schoeneberg",
"treptow-koepenick"
]

years = range(2014,2023)

# download all name files from Berlin open data portal
all_names = []

for borough in boroughs:
    for year in years:
        url = base_url.format(year, borough)
        filename = os.path.join(basedir, "{}-{}.csv".format(year,borough))
        urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, filename)
        df_vornamen_stadtteil = pd.read_csv(filename, sep=',', on_bad_lines='skip')
        df_vornamen_stadtteil['borough'] = borough
        df_vornamen_stadtteil['year'] = year
        all_names.append(df_vornamen_stadtteil)

# concatenate DataFrames
all_names_df = pd.concat(all_names, sort=True)
